issue_title: repodata.json in Channel and Package Metadata are out of Sync/Differ
issue_body:
I have noticed that the metadata, specifcally regarding package dependencies, for some particular packages in the channel repodata.json differs from the metadata in the 'info/index.json'  file that is in the package. Two examples I have encountered are the 'pip' packagte, and the 'matplotlib' package. 

What this means is the dependency tree that is built when online (which uses the channel repodata.json) will differ when running offline, which (I believe) uses the index.json within packages. I believe this is the cause of several other bugs which are posted (selection that I quickly found): #1768, #1660 

So it appears that the metadata is being updated in the repodata.json on the official conda channel, but the packages are not being rebuilt with the newer, updated metadata. 

Here's an example, of the 'pip' package: 

From the repodata.json file at: https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/linux-64/repodata.json

```
"pip-7.1.2-py27_0.tar.bz2": {
  "build": "py27_0",
  "build_number": 0,
  "date": "2015-08-24",
  "depends": [
    "python 2.7*",
    "setuptools",
    "wheel"
  ],
  "license": "MIT",
  "md5": "71c111b98dfa27bb8d8a727a604ff0b2",
  "name": "pip",
  "requires": [],
  "size": 1434745,
  "version": "7.1.2"
},
```

And here is the index.json file: 

```
{
  "arch": "x86_64",
  "build": "py27_0",
  "build_number": 0,
  "depends": [
    "python 2.7*",
    "setuptools",
    "wheel 0.24.0"
  ],
  "license": "MIT",
  "name": "pip",
  "platform": "linux",
  "requires": [
    "python 2.7",
    "setuptools 18.1",
    "wheel 0.24.0"
  ],
  "subdir": "linux-64",
  "ucs": 4,
  "version": "7.1.2"
}
```

You'll see that the requirements are different, the repodata.json states only that pip requires on wheel, but the index.json file in the actual pip package requires wheel version 0.24.0. 

Matplotlib has a similar issue where the requirements have differing version number requirements (freetype 2.5.2 is required by the index.json while the repodata.json claims 2.5.*)

So, I believe this bug is related to conda, but is really an issue of the packages that are maintained in the official conda channel.

issue_title: Chardet returns ISO-8859 when cp1252 is better
issue_body:
Ages ago, [I filed a bug that got erroneously closed by a commit][1]. I just stumbled on it again today, so I'm moving it over from the old location so we can see about getting it going again. 

Here's the text of the old bug:

> I had a frustrating issue recently when trying to use chardet to work with a web page: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/11588458/how-to-handle-encodings-using-python-requests-library

> My solution was to write a bit of custom code that says, "Whenever chardet reports ISO-8859-1, instead use cp1252." It would be great if chardet internalized this behavior.

> Basically, browsers don't use a number of character encodings, and instead map to other ones instead. Since most of the data that chardet is used on will be coming from the web, it makes sense for it to return the character encodings that are used by browsers. 

> This might make sense as an option rather than default functionality....not sure, but I'd love to see this be added.

Is there still an appetite for this kind of an issue? Basically, I think—and ages ago, committer @dan-blanchard agreed—that chardet should never return ISO-8859 and should always return cp1252 instead.

[1]: https://github.com/dcramer/chardet/issues/8

issue_title: For developers: How to switch to Python 3
issue_body:
This isn't actually an "issue", but here are the instructions for developers to switch to using Python 3:

Having PmagPy in your PATH via the developer install means that your system will access whichever version of a program your git branch is pointing to.  First step will be downloading Anaconda.  There are other Python distributions that provide Python 3 (installing everything yourself using brew, or Enthought's EDM), but Anaconda is the easiest.  I recommend downloading Anaconda Python 3 -- any version of Anaconda will allow you to access various versions of Python 2 and Python 3, but it is nice to have Python 3 as the default.

Anaconda comes with most of the required packages already installed, but you will need to install a few more. 

    # download some non-default Python 3 packages
    conda install future  # might need sudo on some distributions
    # for Mac / Linux:
    sudo -H pip install --upgrade --pre -f https://wxpython.org/Phoenix/snapshot-builds/ wxPython_Phoenix
    # for Windows:
    C:\PATH_TO_PIP\pip.exe install --upgrade --pre -f https://wxpython.org/Phoenix/snapshot-builds/ wxPython_Phoenix
    
Now, you may need to switch back and forth between 2 and 3.  Let's say you have Anaconda installed with python 3 as your default Python, and that you have the developer install already.  If you need to switch back to using Python 2.7 and the Python 2 compatible PmagPy, you can follow these steps:

    # create a Python 2 conda environment (one time only)
    conda create python2_env python=python2.7
    # switch to using that python 2 environment
    source activate python2_env    # for windows: activate python2_env
    # install any needed packages into this environment (one time only)
    # for example:
    conda install numpy
    pip install --upgrade wxpython
    # switch to the Python2 branch (must be in your PmagPy git directory)
    git checkout Python2

To go back to your defaults of Python 3 and PmagPy for Python 3:

    source deactivate  # for windows:  deactivate
    git checkout master

Of course, you can set this up the opposite way -- have Python 2 as your default and switch into a Python 3 environment when needed.

NCP-UART: assert when used with CDC
issue_body:
One of my devices uses USB CDC protocol as a UART channel. This means that before the VCOM is opened on the host, no data can be sent and as such otPlatUartSend returns an error, which causes an assert in `ncp_uart.cpp`, when sending an initial status update (even before wpantund sends any information).

I've changed otPlatUartSend code to always return no error and this seems to work, however I have few questions.
1) Are there any potentials problems (NCP-wise) with otPlatUartSend always returning success, even though if the actual data is not sent (at least initially)?
2) What's the purpose of sending the initial status update before any messages are received from the host? In real UART case there is no way of knowing that the host is listening.

Also this does not look like such a critical area to cause an assert. Here's few ideas how it could be changed:
1) Ignore the returned status of otPlatUartSend.
2) Add flag which enables sending messages only after information is received from host.
3) Add a forth `UartTxState` state `kSendingFrame`, which would retry the otPlatUartSend until success.

Any thoughts?

issue_title: stdout_capturing bytes/str problem
issue_body:
In stdout_capturing, `tee_output_fd` creates a text-mode NamedTemporaryFile (mode `w+`), which makes its `read()` function return a `str` object. However, CapturedStdout then uses the length of the returned str as an input to `seek`, which expects a byte index. This goes wrong when using a multi-byte locale (such as utf-8), since the length of the str will then not necessarily be equal to the number of bytes read from the buffer.

I can see two ways of possibly fixing this:
* Either, set the NamedTemporaryFile mode to `w+b` to make the NamedTemporaryFile return bytes objects instead of str objects. This requires modifying the test suite as well since that currently assumes str output, and I'm not sure if this will have any impact on Windows.
* Or, do `self.read_position = self.buffer.tell()` instead of `self.read_position += len(value)` in `CapturedStdout.get()`

A patch for the second suggestion is at https://github.com/wjp/sacred/commit/a4d33402b3eb8affe4b6c18176e3fe01c60ddcc5 . Ideally I'd also have added a py.test test case for this, but I still need to work out how that handles locale-specific tests.

Tested with sacred git master, and python 3.6, in linux. It can be triggered via a sacred experiment, but this is a small script to directly reproduce it (using a utf-8 encoded python script and locale):

```
from sacred.stdout_capturing import get_stdcapturer

capture_mode, capture_stdout = get_stdcapturer("fd")

with capture_stdout() as x:
  print(u"éé")
  text = x.get()
```

issue_title: Integrate decoder with web speech API
checkpoint_step should be made functional again
issue_body:
In the run from last night we ran into the problem of issue #239, the batch size for the validation sets needs to be smaller than for the training set or an OOM is thrown.

However, in the current code one can not decouple checkpointing and validation runs. Thus one can not checkpoint without validating which currently causes a OOM error.

To partially solve this checkpointing should be decoupled from validating. Validating should checkpoint, but checkpointing should not imply validating.

issue_title: Update tested version
issue_body:
Currently, tox.ini has:

    envlist = {py26}-{14,15,16},{py27}-{14,15,16,17},{py33,py34}-{15,16,17},docs,flake8

We should probably drop official support for Python 2.6 entirely, and start testing Django 1.8 and 1.9. We should also probably stop testing Django 1.4-1.7, since those are also EOL (see [the docs](https://www.djangoproject.com/download/#supported-versions)). We should also start testing with Python 3.5.

This will help with work on #63.

issue_title: special characters within parameters are made html safe
issue_body:
Mustache appears to be doing some escaping on parameters by default.

The following was passed as a parameter:

```
jdbc:postgresql://localhost:5432/database?ssl=true&sslfactory=org.postgresql.ssl.NonValidatingFactory
```

It was then used as an environment variable:

```
TaupageConfig:
    environment:
        JDBC_URL: "{{Arguments.jdbcUrl}}"
```

In the instance userdata script the following was seen:

```
jdbc:postgresql://localhost:5432/database?ssl=true&sslfactory=org.postgresql.ssl.NonValidatingFactory
```

Note the `&` which resulted in the application failing to deploy.

Workaround for now, was to use `JDBC_URL: "{{{Arguments.jdbcUrl}}}"` as the environment variable not the three braces instead of two. Didn't see this workaround documented anywhere.

issue_title: `.type` is not always set
issue_body:
``` python
awacs.aws.Policy(Version='2012-10-17', Statement=[])  # No error
awacs.aws.Policy(Version='2012-10-17', statement=[])  # Try to raise on line 84, but .type is never set, so we raise on line 46 
```

GitHub's storage layer is down but here is a patch that I presume does the right thing.

``` diff
--- a/awacs/__init__.py 2015-10-18 15:35:38.000000000 -0700
+++ b/awacs/__init__.py 2015-10-18 15:40:23.000000000 -0700
@@ -20,6 +20,10 @@
         self.props = props
         # Cache the keys for validity checks
         self.propnames = props.keys()
+        if type is None:
+            self.type = type(self).__module__ + '.' + type(self).__name__
+        else:
+            self.type = type

         # unset/None is also legal
         if name and not valid_names.match(name):
```

issue_title: Bundle ZMQ in manylinux wheels?
issue_body:
In my conintuing silliness of mixing pyzmq and libzmq via Cython, I've noticed that when the manylinux wheel is installed, it effectively bundles the libzmq library, in ``zmq/.libs/``, which makes it difficult to compile other python extensions linked against the libzmq in use by pyzmq. (This is done by the manylinux wheel machinery)

Explicitly bundling libzmq when building manylinux would make it much easier to link against pyzmq's copy of libzmq. Otherwise I just force pyzmq to never use binaries, but this seems silly when the solution is already built into pyzmq.

issue_title: unexpected keyword argument error on JSON serialization
issue_body:
After `simplejson` usage was removed in #780, the [serialization error handling](https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/blob/master/raven/utils/json.py#L50) no longer works as expected, because the standard lib `json.JSONEncoder.__init__()` does not accept the `encoding` keyword argument, producing `unexpected keyword argument` error.

In addition to that, `simplejson.dumps` can handle bytestring keys in dicts, while `json.dumps` can't: https://bugs.python.org/issue18820. 
E. g. (with Python 3.4):
```python
>>> simplejson.dumps({b't': 1})
'{"t": 1}'
>>> json.dumps({b't': 1})
...
TypeError: keys must be a string
```

issue_title: unable to play 4.4.0 65895 replays using cmd-line client + protocol
issue_body:
### SITUATION:
I wanted to extract some game information from replays from WCS Valencia 2018 using Blizzard's [API protocol](https://github.com/Blizzard/s2client-proto).  However, it appears an automatically downloaded Battle.net cache does not sufficiently enable a manual launch of the replay using an older version of the game client which allows protocol connections.

- The replay pack was downloaded and extracted from [the official WCS.battle.net site](https://wcs.starcraft2.com/en-us/news/21965429/Replay-Pack:-2018-WCS-Valencia/).
- These replays are versioned as 4.4.0.65895.  Using the battle.net-connected client that relaunches the replay offline, I can load and view these replays without issue.
- I can review other replays and extract its game data [using this protocol](https://github.com/Blizzard/s2client-proto/blob/master/docs/protocol.md) without issue (provided I already have the corresponding map version).
- I have tried on Windows 10 and Windows 7 machines; both produce this issue.  I haven't tried other OS.
- There doesn't appear to be any issue with the 65895 version, the API connection or socket communication in other replays or games.  Both matches and replays run normally with observation requests returning valid data.

### PROBLEM STATEMENT:

Cannot play older replays using an older version of the SC2 game client manually launched offline with a valid connection to a compatible API client.

### ERROR MESSAGE:

SC2APIProtocol.ResponseStartReplay.Error.LaunchError: 'The launch of this game makes reference to mod or map dependencies which are no longer available.'

### STEPS TO REPRODUCE:

1. remove existing Battle.net folder from the main SC2 application folder.
2. Modify the command-line launch with the `-PrePopulateCache` option per [these instructions](https://github.com/Blizzard/s2client-proto/issues/12).
  * Adding this option on the command line directly when launching a Base***** version also launches the downloader application, but it crashes.
3. Place all WCS 2018 - Valencia replays in the main SC2 application folder \ Replays.  (For good measure, I also placed the replay files inside the `C:\Users\ME\Documents\StarCraft II\Accounts\#######\1-S2-1-####\Replays\Multiplayer` folder where other replays are located.)
4. Click 'Play' on the Blizzard game launcher.
  * A new window appears informing that additional downloads are required.
  * Game data is downloaded successfully; the window indicates as such.
5. Execute the following command:
`C:/Program Files (x86)/StarCraft II\Versions\Base65895\SC2_x64.exe -listen 127.0.0.1 -port 16857 -dataDir C:/Program Files (x86)/StarCraft II\ -tempDir C:\Users\ME\AppData\Local\Temp\sc-a0tio9gx\ -displayMode 1 -dataVersion BF41339C22AE2EDEBEEADC8C75028F7D`
  * the Base65895 version is downloaded previously when viewing a replay that was played with this game version -- i.e. a WCS Valencia 2018 replay.
  * this exact command works just fine when supplying a different replay in the StartReplay request.
  * the replay is able to launch when double clicking the .SC2Replay file directly.

issue_title: Supporting Airspeed Velocity
issue_body:
In general, regarding Airspeed Velocity (regression testing) - In order to run and log a benchmark, suite it will build by calling `setup.py develop` from the root directory (where `.git` resides). A few complications arise here:
  - `setup.py` for `ray` does not sit in the root directory
  - building from source can take a while, especially given that we already have wheels built and pushed to s3. 

There’s a couple workarounds (ie, having a separate benchmarking repository) but would be nice to hear thoughts.

## Plan:

- [x] Get an initial commit for ASV into the repo (#2113). This will only support `asv dev`.
- [x] Setup ASV so that build functionality is not completely encapsulated in `asv run`. This may require forking ASV into `ray-project`.
   - [x] Include a `machine.json` in Docker so that asv can run without input

3. Get a full docker + ASV pipeline working
 - [x] Expose Env Vars in Docker
 - [x] Push results to a proper directory structure in S3
 - [x] Make sure visualization works correctly (right now only visualizes one commit)

Optimizations:
 - [ ] Make the jenkins build faster


cc @robertnishihara @pcmoritz

issue_title: Env variables for ANDROIDAPI ignored
issue_body:
Build.py will just use the default from the argparser instead of overwriting it with the shell variable value or using that value as the default in the argparser.

Quick fixes in either AndroidManifest.xml.tmpl or build.py but I don't want to screw up buildozer's expected behavior or I'd patch it myself.

In AndroidManifest.xml.tmpl:
`<uses-sdk android:minSdkVersion="{{ args.min_sdk_version }}" androi...`

In build.py:
```python
    # Get target android API
    android_api = int(os.environ.get('ANDROIDAPI', '8'))
```

`args.min_sdk_version` isn't being overwritten by the shell variable and the shell variable isn't used as the default value in the arg parser.  It would seem that shell variables are less specific than args, but really why isn't the API version hard-coded into each build.py considering that the SDK is set during distribute.sh?

issue_title: .tostring becoming deprecated in python3
issue_body:
So I have been running python-xlib for quite som time, and since my distribution upgraded to python3.6 i have gotten this message very often

```
/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/python_xlib-0.19-py3.6.egg/Xlib/protocol/rq.py:1012: DeprecationWarning: tostring() is deprecated. Use tobytes() instead.
```

So today i decided to fix it. Was an easy fix, here's the diff

```
diff --git a/Xlib/protocol/rq.py b/Xlib/protocol/rq.py
index c945c62..11ea61c 100644
--- a/Xlib/protocol/rq.py
+++ b/Xlib/protocol/rq.py
@@ -553,7 +553,7 @@ class List(ValueField):
             if self.type.check_value is not None:
                 val = [self.type.check_value(v) for v in val]
             data = array(struct_to_array_codes[self.type.structcode],
-                               val).tostring()
+                               val).tobytes()
         else:
             data = []
             for v in val:
@@ -685,7 +685,7 @@ class PropertyData(ValueField):
                 val = list(val)
 
             size = fmt // 8
-            data = array(array_unsigned_codes[size], val).tostring()
+            data = array(array_unsigned_codes[size], val).tobytes()
             dlen = len(val)
 
         dl = len(data)
@@ -806,7 +806,7 @@ class KeyboardMapping(ValueField):
             for i in range(len(v), keycodes):
                 a.append(X.NoSymbol)
 
-        return a.tostring(), len(value), keycodes
+        return a.tobytes(), len(value), keycodes
 
 
 class ModifierMapping(ValueField):
@@ -837,7 +837,7 @@ class ModifierMapping(ValueField):
             for i in range(len(v), keycodes):
                 a.append(0)
 
-        return a.tostring(), len(value), keycodes
+        return a.tobytes(), len(value), keycodes
 
 class EventField(ValueField):
     structcode = None
```

So why did I did I not make a PR you might ask? Personally i only use python3 where the unit tests passed, but when i ran the unit tests under python2 things wasn't looking as good

```
54 failed, 463 passed, 406 skipped
```

I'm not really sure what to do about it, but atleast I'll create this issue so it won't be forgotten.

issue_title: HdfsReadPipe silently fails when unable to read

E.g. when a HdfsTarget's format is Plain but the path points to a directory, read() returns an empty string and just the HDFS client's stderr is manifesting as a trace of failure:
```
>>> from luigi.hdfs import HdfsTarget
>>> t = HdfsTarget('/foo/bar/2016-07-20')
/src/luigi/luigi/parameter.py:234: UserWarning: Parameter None is not of type string.
  warnings.warn("Parameter {0} is not of type string.".format(str(x)))
>>> s = t.open().read(1)
cat: `/foo/bar/2016-07-20': Is a directory
>>> s
''
```
The expected behavior would be an exception. The actual CLI clients (`hadoop fs` or `snakebite` that we tried) do exit with error return codes:
```
$ snakebite cat /foo/bar/2016-07-20
cat: `/foo/bar/2016-07-20': Is a directory
$ echo $?
255
$ hadoop fs -cat /foo/bar/2016-07-20
cat: `/foo/bar/2016-07-20': Is a directory

1
```

issue_title: If a worker times out and the task is marked as "FAILED", the history still shows it as "RUNNING"
issue_body:
Steps:
- Start a task via command line that will take a while
- Wait a few seconds (so it registers with the scheduler), then kill the task (control+C)
- Wait 60 seconds
- Go to the scheduler UI/API - the task shows as "FAILED", and the logs show "INFO: Worker Worker(...) timed out (no contact for >=60.0s)"
- HOWEVER - if you go to /history, the status shows as "RUNNING" and is never updated
- Even if you restart luigid, the task will forever show as RUNNING in /history

Looks like the history feature is not properly being updated, even though the UI is.

(EDIT: luigi 2.1.1)

issue_title: Ability to specify last_modified value when updating records
issue_body:
One current limitation of the current protocol is that fact that the `last_modified` metadata (on buckets, collections and records) cannot be set from an external source.

For instance, if one wants replicate the data between two instances (origin and destination), when pushing the updates, the `last_modified` field from the origin data will be replaced by a new one defined by the destination.

As such, the data between the two instances cannot be considered equal.

One way to fix this would be to let the client define what the `last_modified` field is, checking for some heuristics: the new `last-modified` cannot be less than the `max(collection)` for instance.

hi, 

Im running letsencrypt-auto on Debian 8 server with Apache 2.4. We are hosting over a hundred domains and the experience with the domain selection is kinda frustrating:
- the domains are show in a very small window, enabling to see only the next 5/6 domains at once
- the domains are not ordered alphabetically but in some weird garbled order (maybe by .conf creaton time ?) This makes the navigation very cumbersome.
- It would be definitely better if the domains shown would be the domains that are enabled in apache - eg. symlinks in the "sites-enabled" directory as compared to the files in the "sites-available" directory as it is now.
- i understand Lets Encrypt wants to push for more web encryption, but maybe a better default for first run would be to have all the domains unchecked, rather then checked. Especially if its a first time user, and hosts many domains, he might spend some considerable time unchecking all he doesnt want to encrypt. Maybe a solution would be a option to "check / uncheck all". 

thank you for you consideration and great work !

using the under sampling function like the example below:

rus = RandomUnderSampler(ratio = 0.3, random_state=0)
 x_rus, y_rus = rus.fit_sample(x_train, y_train)
``` 
I found the majority class rows were being duplicated though there were plenty of data to choose from.  I have only 10% of minority class in my data and using ratio = 0.3, there's plenty of majority class rows to use so why would the RandomUnderSampler duplicate rows in the majority class? rus = RandomUnderSampler(ratio = 0.2, random_state=0)
x_rus, y_rus = down_sampling(x_ds, y_ds, 0.3)
print x_rus

issue_title: Buried type error in exception case in sushi_bar_client
issue_body:
Whilst trying to get a ricecooker up and running, I hit a chain of issues which might be worth fixing.

https://github.com/learningequality/ricecooker/blob/fb625eca06b1e6f803c4a8f7d6c64c3183f1625d/ricecooker/sushi_bar_client.py#L91

assumes `.__config_logger()` returns two items. But it returns `None` if `.run_id` is falsey.

https://github.com/learningequality/ricecooker/blob/fb625eca06b1e6f803c4a8f7d6c64c3183f1625d/ricecooker/sushi_bar_client.py#L160-L162

`.run_id` is the return value of `.__create_channel_run`

https://github.com/learningequality/ricecooker/blob/fb625eca06b1e6f803c4a8f7d6c64c3183f1625d/ricecooker/sushi_bar_client.py#L89

which has a broad sweeping exception which will silence the exception and return None.

(The exception in this case was a 400 for the post  'https://sushibar.learningequality.org/api/channelruns' -- will dig deeper separately; suspect API key wrong.)

I suggest `.__create_channel_run` possibly shouldn't silence the exception at all since it's probably impossible to recover gracefully from, and it should just crash ASAP to avoid later type errors. (It might be useful elsewhere).

public score:2.08153 ,private score 2.44852.  This kaggle project used the log loss as the metric.  why there  are too much big gap between the mxnet local kpi and kaggle ?   Firstly, I guessed the labels were wrong in test data. But I tried to check many times  and found the labels were right.  Secondly, I rewrite my codes in other models ( mlp , cnn ) and train them. So I guess it is overfitting for my local model.

In fact , I did many times for the same test  in Keras( tensorflow backend).  I used the resnet50 model to train the same  pictures.  In training , the Keras kpi can tell me the model is overfitting after several epoches. If I got a 0.68 log loss in keras, then I will get the closer score( 0.7) in Kaggle. It is reliable. 

In the end, I must say that the training kpi in mxnet are very unreliable. My model is too overfitting ,but the training kpi say everything is ok  and just go on.  Is it a big bug ?

issue_title: Add configuration option in airflow.cfg for Flask APPLICATION_ROOT
issue_body:
For various reasons, I might not have the flexibility to use a domain or subdomain root, e.g., `airflow.myorg.com`) as the entry point to Airflow. Instead I may have to use something like `myorg.com/airflow/`.

Fortunately Flask has the `APPLICATION_ROOT` configuration option which could help me enable this access mode for the Airflow webserver easily, [explained here](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/18967441/add-a-prefix-to-all-flask-routes).

I believe this feature is added in two steps:

1. Add configuration option logic to flow from airflow.cfg to instantiation of Flask.
2. Go through existing template files and convert to using `url_for` (e.g. [here](https://github.com/airbnb/airflow/blob/f8046512172f37ffde72fa99d787fc0e609f70ab/airflow/contrib/plugins/metastore_browser/templates/metastore_browser/base.html#L17)).
3. (hidden step) Encourage other contributors to use `url_for` going forward.

Thanks for this project!
